summary: I come from Switzerland, a country with diverse regions and cultures. In the northeast, near my village close to Saint Gall, the landscape is mountainous and beautiful, with pine forests and deep canyons. The people here have distinct traditions, especially in the canton of Appenzell, where the Reformation split the population into Catholic and Protestant communities. Switzerland’s musical heritage is strong, especially with the famous church bells that ring across the villages.

Appenzell Innerrhoden is a unique canton in the extreme northeast of Switzerland where my family has roots. The people there are quite different from the rest of Switzerland, possibly descended from ancient immigrants from the Adriatic coast or even the Huns. The Reformation divided the canton into Catholic and Protestant parts, each with distinct temperaments—the Protestants are practical and businesslike, while the Catholics are more carefree and love to dance. I remember hearing stories from my cousin and friends like Trudl Schoop, who taught in the Pestalozzi Village and witnessed the wild dances in Appenzell.

My cousin, a historian, once told me that the people of Appenzell might have immigrated from the Adriatic coast long ago, possibly before Roman times. This theory suggests that their distinct racial origin and culture in Switzerland could be traced back to that region. It’s fascinating to think that the ancestors of these mountain people traveled such a distance to settle in the high forest lands of Switzerland.

The Reformation was a pivotal event that split the canton of Appenzell into two parts: Catholic and Protestant. This division shaped the character and lifestyle of the people there, with Protestants being practical and business-minded, and Catholics being more carefree and sociable. The religious split influenced not only beliefs but also social customs, humor, and even the way communities interacted with each other in Switzerland.

I once compared the stature of the Protestant people in Appenzell to that of the Japanese—they are both rather short in height. This comparison came up when I was describing the practical and somewhat stubborn nature of the Protestants there. It’s interesting how physical traits can remind you of people from faraway places like Japan.

Trudl Schoop is a woman I know who once lived in Appenzell and taught at the Pestalozzi Village. She told me about the wild dances in the Catholic part of Appenzell, describing them as the roughest she had ever seen—wilder even than dances in the Congo. Her stories gave me a vivid picture of the lively and spirited culture in that region.

The Pestalozzi Village is where Trudl Schoop was teaching when she lived in Appenzell. It’s a place connected to education and community life in that region. Through Trudl’s experiences there, I learned about the vibrant social life in Appenzell, especially the energetic dances in the Catholic part of the canton.

My village is just a few miles from the city of Saint Gallen, which is a center of civilization compared to the surrounding countryside. From Saint Gallen, if you travel uphill for about an hour, you reach the beautiful Appenzell region with its hills and deep canyons. Saint Gallen is part of the cultural and geographic backdrop of my life in northeast Switzerland.

From the hills near my village in Switzerland, you can see across the border into Germany. The view stretches toward Lake Constance and the lower canton of Thurgau, with Germany lying just beyond. It’s a reminder of how close our countries are and how the landscape connects us.

Lake Constance is a beautiful sight from the hills near my home in Switzerland. Looking out over the lake, I often wondered about the countries and lands beyond it, especially the fertile lowlands where wheat and grapes grow. It was a place I longed to visit as a child, and my father’s stories about it made it seem like a wonderful world.

Thurgau is a canton I can see from the hills near my village in Switzerland. It lies near Lake Constance and Saint Gallen, forming part of the picturesque landscape of the region. Thurgau represents the lower, more fertile lands compared to the mountainous areas where I grew up.

To the east of my village in Switzerland, you can see the mountains of Vorarlberg, which is part of Austria. The mountain views from my home stretch across borders and give a sense of the vast alpine region we live in. Vorarlberg’s peaks are a constant presence in the landscape I grew up with.

I have noticed that the church bells in Italy are quite different from those in Switzerland. While Swiss villages have beautifully tuned sets of bells, Italian bells tend to be louder and less musical, just banging away. This contrast made me appreciate the musical pride of my homeland even more.

The Rhine Valley lies below the hills near my home, and it’s where many of the vegetables we couldn’t grow in the mountains came from. I was fortunate to spend some time there on a farm during school vacation, which was one of the most wonderful experiences of my life. The valley’s fertile land and warmer climate were very different from the apple and pear orchards of my village.
